Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered throughout the course. I tried to set aside about eight hours a week, but with six online courses running simultaneously, I had to jump between subjects constantly. I'm someone who learns best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. I didn't have a strict schedule written down—I just tried to fit studying into whatever gaps I could find in my day.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram in more hours, but during quieter weeks, I might only check in briefly. Being 26 and having other responsibilities means I can't always stick to a perfect routine. I tried to be consistent, but life has a way of throwing things off track, and I just had to adapt as I went along.
Q3: Usually it was assignment deadlines that got me to log in. I'd get a notification or remember something was due, and that would push me to check the LMS. Sometimes I logged in just to see if there were any announcements or if grades had been posted. To be honest, I wasn't logging in daily—maybe once a week on average—so it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: I wouldn't say using the LMS ever became a natural habit for me. It always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do rather than an automatic part of my day. The gamified tasks they introduced did make it slightly more interesting, and I found myself curious about earning points or badges sometimes. But overall, the routine never really stuck because I was juggling too many courses and the platform wasn't where I spent most of my actual study time.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner—I absorb information much better when I hear it explained. I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, and let the concepts sink in through repetition. The discussion posts were also helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. Those resources felt like they were actually teaching me something rather than just checking a box.
Q6: I tended to skip over the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. It's not that they weren't valuable, but reading on a screen for long periods doesn't work well for me. I also minimally engaged with some of the optional quizzes because I was already stretched thin with required work across all my courses. When you're managing six courses, you have to make choices about where to invest your limited time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to the assignments or exams. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with or gave me practice for what I'd be tested on, I was all in. The gamified elements actually surprised me—I didn't expect to care about points and badges, but they did make certain tasks feel more rewarding. Anything that felt like busy work without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd sacrifice when time got tight. Required activities had deadlines and consequences, so they automatically got priority. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to it later' and then later never came. It's not that I didn't see their value; I just couldn't justify spending time on them when I had mandatory work piling up across multiple courses.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download the lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while exercising. I also used YouTube to find additional explanations of difficult concepts—sometimes a different teacher's perspective makes things click. My notes were handwritten in a physical notebook, and I'd review those constantly without ever touching the platform.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded audio of lectures while doing other tasks—that's real learning for me, but the system only saw that I watched the video once. I also discussed course material with a study partner over WhatsApp, working through problems together. None of that peer collaboration showed up in my official peer interaction count on the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture once online, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. I'd review my notes or replay the audio file dozens of times, but the system only registered that single initial view. For someone who learns through repetition and listening, my actual engagement was way higher than what the platform recorded.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult. My anxiety would spike around exam time, and even though I'm generally resilient, that mental pressure made it hard to focus. Sometimes I'd sit down to study and just feel overwhelmed by how much material there was across all six courses. During those moments, I'd have to step back and remind myself that I've overcome challenges before—that's what kept me going.
Q13: The workload across six online courses was honestly brutal at times. When multiple deadlines converged, I had to triage and prioritize which courses got my attention. Fatigue was real—by the end of some weeks, I was mentally exhausted and my LMS usage would drop significantly. I tried to push through, but there were times when I just couldn't engage meaningfully because my brain was fried from competing responsibilities.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxiety about exams or other deadlines, and the words would just wash over me. I'd technically be 'watching' a video but thinking about something else entirely. Those sessions felt wasted, and I'd have to come back later when I could actually focus properly.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't sure where to start. My anxiety sometimes made me avoid tasks that I knew were important because facing them felt too stressful. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace, but tomorrow often brought its own challenges. It's something I struggle with—knowing something matters but still putting it off because of that anxious feeling in my chest.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the platform—downloading materials, submitting assignments, checking requirements. Without that external pressure, I tended to drift and focus on other things. It's not the healthiest approach, but when you're managing six courses, deadlines become the only reliable way to prioritize what needs attention right now.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to stay engaged and try harder. But when feedback was critical or when I saw my exam scores weren't where I wanted them to be, it triggered my anxiety and sometimes made me want to disengage temporarily. I'd have to work through those feelings before I could productively use the feedback to improve. The gap between my assignment scores and exam scores was particularly frustrating and confusing.
Q18: The course structure definitely influenced how I participated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to focus on, but the sheer volume of content across six courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single one. The gamified tasks were a nice touch—they broke up the monotony and gave me small wins to celebrate. But overall, the structure assumed I had more time to dedicate than I actually did.
Q19: The video-based content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner—I could actually absorb information that way. The gamification elements also helped by making tasks feel less like chores. What made it harder was the amount of reading required and the fact that discussion forums felt a bit disconnected from the main learning. Also, the LMS interface wasn't always intuitive, which added friction when I was already short on time.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are searching for something specific or trying to understand requirements. You might click through a lot of materials looking for answers but never actually complete the work because you're confused or overwhelmed. It could also be anxiety-driven—checking the platform repeatedly without being able to commit to doing the actual tasks. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggling rather than engaging productively.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS activity was low—I logged in maybe once a week—but I was studying quite a bit offline through downloaded materials and audio lectures. Someone looking at my login frequency would think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in real effort in ways the system couldn't see. The platform only captures a slice of what learning actually looks like, especially for people who don't learn best by sitting at a computer.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show things like submission patterns, deadline compliance, and which resources students click on. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline study, discussions with peers outside the platform, or the mental effort someone puts into understanding difficult concepts. They definitely can't measure things like anxiety or motivation that hugely affect how students engage.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for the sake of it. It's when I'm curious about a concept and want to dig deeper, or when I'm actively thinking about how ideas connect rather than just memorizing facts. True engagement is mental investment—it's being present and focused, not just physically showing up. It's something internal that doesn't always show up in external metrics.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how hard I had to fight through mental barriers to engage with the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures on repeat while commuting, or the late-night conversations with study partners working through problems together. It also wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept pushing forward despite failing the exam, or how the gamified tasks actually surprised me by making things more enjoyable. The human side of learning just doesn't fit into data points.
